mnemonics: Once Virtual, Always Virtual (OVAV): A virtual function in a base class stays virtual in all derived classes, even without the 'virtual' keyword.
The Family Name: Think of a virtual function as a family name. All children inherit it, and you don't need to re-state the last name for every child.
keyPoints: The 'virtual' attribute of a member function is inherited by any function that overrides it in a derived class.
It is not necessary, but considered good practice, to use the 'virtual' keyword in the derived class's function declaration.
The 'override' keyword (C++11) should be used to explicitly state intent and allow the compiler to catch errors.
A function declared as 'virtual' in the base class will be virtual through the entire inheritance hierarchy.
commonMistakes: Re-declaring the function as 'virtual' in every derived class, which is redundant and can clutter the code.
Forgetting that the function is still virtual and expecting non-virtual behavior (like early binding) when called through a base class pointer.
Assuming a function is virtual in a derived class because the base class function is virtual, but accidentally hiding it by changing the function signature (name, parameters, or const-ness).
